pi_items.source: Task description explicitly states: 'Save your project as cam_profile.ggb in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/'

Task description explicitly states: 'The Base Circle Radius is 3 units.' Base circle radius is the standard foundation for cam design in mechanical engineering (verified against https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/cam-profile and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cam_(mechanism))

Task description explicitly states: 'Rise 2 units using Simple Harmonic Motion' in the 90°-180° phase. Simple Harmonic Motion is a standard, well-documented approach for cam rise and return motion (verified against https://www.sathyabama.ac.in/sites/default/files/course-material/2020-10/UNIT-III.pdf)

All commands verified against GeoGebra official documentation: (1) Curve - parametric equations command (https://geogebra.github.io/docs/manual/en/commands/Curve/), (2) If - conditional logic command (https://geogebra.github.io/docs/manual/en/commands/If/), (3) cos and sin - built-in trigonometric functions (https://www.houseofmath.com/geogebra/geometry/trigonometry/how-to-use-trigonometric-functions-in-geogebra). Task description explicitly requires: 'parametric curve (Curve command)', 'piecewise displacement function (If command)', and 'polar-to-cartesian conversion equations: x = (R + f(t)) * cos(t), y = (R + f(t)) * sin(t)'

privileged_info_summary: All metadata claims are verified as accurate. The task specifies designing a radial plate cam profile in GeoGebra with a base circle radius of 3.0 units and maximum follower lift of 2.0 units. The displacement sequence uses Simple Harmonic Motion for rise and return phases (both standard mechanical engineering practices). The required GeoGebra commands (Curve for parametric equations, If for piecewise logic, cos/sin for polar-to-cartesian conversion) are correctly specified and all exist as documented GeoGebra functions. The expected output filename 'cam_profile.ggb' is correctly specified. This is a synthetic task with no external data sources - all parameters are task-derived specifications for a parametric curve generation exercise.
